{{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|ja|桜井市|12=draft}}, Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Group|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|12=draft}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=draft}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山|12=draft}}. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Group," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group|12=draft}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|12=draft}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|12=draft}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼|12=draft}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|ja|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|12=draft}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳|12=draft}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳|12=draft}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳|12=draft}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higaida Otsuka Kofun|ja|東田大塚古墳|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|12=draft}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳|12=draft}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|12=draft}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|12=draft}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higaida Otsuka Kofun|ja|東田大塚古墳|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|12=draft}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|12=draft}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

revision.text:
{{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|ja|桜井市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Group|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=draft|12=draft}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Group," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group|12=draft|12=draft}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼|12=draft|12=draft}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft|12=draft}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|ja|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higaida Otsuka Kofun|ja|東田大塚古墳|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|12=draft|12=draft}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higaida Otsuka Kofun|ja|東田大塚古墳|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|12=draft|12=draft}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|纒向型前方後円墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * [[List of large kofun in Japan]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higaida Otsuka Kofun|ja|東田大塚古墳|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

revision.text:
{{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|ja|桜井市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai, Nara|qq=}}, Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Group|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Miwa|qq=}}. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Group," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himiko|qq=}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|ja|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|qq=}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higaida Otsuka Kofun|ja|東田大塚古墳|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Otsuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higaida Otsuka Kofun|ja|東田大塚古墳|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Otsuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=}} * {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku ruins|qq=}} * {{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|纒向型前方後円墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|qq=}} * [[List of large kofun in Japan]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himiko|qq=}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|ja|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|qq=}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higaida Otsuka Kofun|ja|東田大塚古墳|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Otsuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higaida Otsuka Kofun|ja|東田大塚古墳|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Otsuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=}} * {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku ruins|qq=}} * {{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns|lt=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns|lt=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|纒向型前方後円墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|qq=}} * [[List of large kofun in Japan]]

{{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|ja|桜井市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai, Nara|qq=}}, Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Group|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|ja|三輪山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Miwa|qq=}}. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Group," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himiko|qq=}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|ja|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|qq=}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|纒向石塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|纒向矢塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|纒向勝山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higaida Otsuka Kofun|ja|東田大塚古墳|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Otsuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|ホケノ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higaida Otsuka Kofun|ja|東田大塚古墳|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Otsuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Group|qq=}} * {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}} * {{ill|Makimuku ruins|ja|纒向遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku ruins|qq=}} * {{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns|lt=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns|lt=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|纒向型前方後円墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|qq=}} * [[List of large kofun in Japan]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Ōyamato Kofun Group" to "Yamato Kofun Group"
revision.text:
{{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kofun Group|qq=}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Yamato Kofun Group," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Yamato Kofun Group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kofun Group|qq=}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]], is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] manages it as the burial site of [[:en:Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]].
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]] : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]] : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]]|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamato Kofun Group|ja|大和古墳群|1=Yamato Kofun Group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kofun Group|qq=}} * {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * {{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns|lt=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns|lt=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|纒向型前方後円墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|qq=}} * [[List of large kofun in Japan]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{Kofun navbar}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Sakurai, Nara]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:kofun clusters]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] [[Category:Makimuku Kofun Group|*]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]] [[Category:Yamato Kofun Group]]
}}

== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]]|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

revision.text:
{{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]], is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] manages it as the burial site of [[:en:Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]].
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]] : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]] : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]]|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=}} * {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}} * [[:en:Makimuku ruins|Makimuku ruins]] * {{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns|lt=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns|lt=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|纒向型前方後円墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|qq=}} * [[List of large kofun in Japan]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

}}

; [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]]|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

{{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]], is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] manages it as the burial site of [[:en:Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]].
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]] : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]] : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]]|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]] : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]] : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]]|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Makimuku Kofun Cluster
revision.text:
{{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]], is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] manages it as the burial site of [[:en:Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]].
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]] : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]] : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]]|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]], is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] manages it as the burial site of [[:en:Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]].
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped Kofuns" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]] : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]] : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]]|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}
