<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Sakurai|WD=Q127026}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.54669444444445|N|135.83605555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11608504}}|part_of={{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420}}; {{ill|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075}}; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664}}; {{ill|Izuka Kofun|WD=Q11286816}}; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505}}; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502}}; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|WD=Q11608503}}|Historic Site={{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11608501]] Wikidata name: Makimuku Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]], is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] manages it as the burial site of [[:en:Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]].
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; [[:en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]] : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; [[:en:Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; [[:en:Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun]] : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]] : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; [[:en:Hokenoyama Kofun|Hokenoyama Kofun]] : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | [[:en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]]|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}}, Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|WD=Q11387098|lt=Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505|lt=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|WD=Q11608503|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|en|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664|lt=Hokenoyama Kofun}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Sakurai|WD=Q127026}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.54669444444445|N|135.83605555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11608504}}|part_of={{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420}}; {{ill|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075}}; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664}}; {{ill|Izuka Kofun|WD=Q11286816}}; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505}}; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502}}; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|WD=Q11608503}}|Historic Site={{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11608501]] Wikidata name: Makimuku Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}}, Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|WD=Q11387098|lt=Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505|lt=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|WD=Q11608503|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|en|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664|lt=Hokenoyama Kofun}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}}, Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|WD=Q11387098|lt=Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505|lt=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|WD=Q11608503|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|en|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664|lt=Hokenoyama Kofun}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|WD=Q11387098|lt=Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505|lt=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|WD=Q11608503|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|en|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664|lt=Hokenoyama Kofun}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. ; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|en|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664|lt=Hokenoyama Kofun}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Sakurai|WD=Q127026}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.54669444444445|N|135.83605555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11608504}}|part_of={{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420}}; {{ill|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075}}; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664}}; {{ill|Izuka Kofun|WD=Q11286816}}; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505}}; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502}}; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|WD=Q11608503}}|Historic Site={{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}}} {{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}}, Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|WD=Q11387098|lt=Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505|lt=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|WD=Q11608503|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|en|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664|lt=Hokenoyama Kofun}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}. The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.

It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."

; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505|lt=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|1=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>

The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster]][[ja:纒向古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Sakurai|WD=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.54669444444445|N|135.83605555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11608504|1=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun}}|part_of={{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|1=Hashihaka Kofun}}; {{ill|Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}}; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664|1=Hokenoyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Izuka Kofun|WD=Q11286816|1=Izuka Kofun}}; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505|1=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun}}; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|1=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|WD=Q11608503|1=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun}}|Historic Site={{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580|1=Historic Site of Japan}}}} {{coord|34|32|48.1|N|135|50|09.8|E|region:JP-29|display=title}} {{Location map |Japan Nara | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 32 | lat_sec = 48.1 | lon_deg = 135 | lon_min = 50 | lon_sec = 09.8 | label = 纒向古墳群 | position = bottom | mark = Red pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 200 | caption = {{center|纒向古墳群}} }}
'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko|1=Himiko}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|WD=Q11387098|lt=Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|1=Yamato-totohi-momoso-hime}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505|lt=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|1=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|WD=Q11608503|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|1=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|1=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|en|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664|lt=Hokenoyama Kofun|1=Hokenoyama Kofun}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

'''Makimuku Kofun Group''' (纒向古墳群, *Makimuku Kofungun*) is an early Kofun period Kofun group located in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, Japan. It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko|1=Himiko}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|WD=Q11387098|lt=Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|1=Yamato-totohi-momoso-hime}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|WD=Q11608505|lt=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|1=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|WD=Q11608503|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|1=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|1=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|en|Hokenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11337664|lt=Hokenoyama Kofun|1=Hokenoyama Kofun}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
== Comparison of the Six Keyhole Mounds == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Name !! Period !! Length (m) !! Notable Artifacts |- | {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Late 2nd-early 3rd century || 96 || Painted wooden figures, arc-patterned boards |- | {{ill|Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Early 3rd century || 96 || |- | {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Wooden sword hilts, fans |- | {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}}|| Early 3rd century || 110 || Pottery, jar coffins |- | {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokenoyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|| Early 3rd century || 80 || Bronze arrowheads, swords, mirrors |- | {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Late 3rd-early 4th century || 278 || Haniwa, distinctive pottery |}

It forms part of the {{ill|Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja|オオヤマト古墳集団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}} (Yamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group) and is situated south of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, spreading along the western foothills of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}. It is considered the birthplace of the keyhole-shaped Kofun (zenpō-kōen-fun).
== Overview == The Makimuku Kofun Group lies within the southeastern Nara Basin and is part of the larger "Ōyamato Kofun Cluster," which is divided into four sub-groups: the {{ill|Kayō Kofun Group|ja|萱生古墳群|lt=Kayō Kofun Group|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayō Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和古墳群}}, Yanagimoto Kofun Group, Makimuku Kofun Group, and {{ill|Shiki Kofun Group|ja|磯城の古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{harvnb|Wada|1992}}</ref> Located near the ancient Makimuku site (纒向遺跡), it is geographically separated from the Yanagimoto Kofun Group by the Tamashiroyama hills.
The most prominent mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|qid=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}}, is regarded as the earliest standardized keyhole-shaped Kofun. Estimated to have been built in the late 3rd to early 4th century, it marks the beginning of the Kofun period in Japanese history. Since the pre-war research of Shinya Kasai, the Hashihaka Kofun has been hypothesized to be the burial site of {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|qid=Q234451|lt=Himiko|1=Himiko}}, the queen of Yamatai mentioned in the *Records of the Wei Dynasty* (*Wei Zhi*). Currently, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} manages it as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|qid=Q11387098|lt=Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|1=Yamato-totohi-momoso-hime}}.
== Characteristics of the Kofun == Aside from the Hashihaka Kofun, five other mounds in the group are referred to as "Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun" (纒向型前方後円墳). These mounds share the following distinctive features: * The frontal section (zenpō) is significantly smaller and lower compared to the rear section (kōen). * The proportions of the mound's overall length, rear section diameter, and frontal section length adhere to a 3:2:1 ratio. * The rear section is not a perfect circle but a flattened sphere, an inverted egg, or an irregular shape. * The frontal section is narrow in mounds with moats.
In contrast, the Hashihaka Kofun, measuring 278 meters in length, is approximately three times larger than the other mounds and has a highly refined structure, including a five-tiered rear section and a plectrum-shaped frontal section. It is often described as the "first royal tomb of the early Yamato Kingship."
== Notable Kofun == [[File:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|240px|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun (top), Makimuku Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Higaida ohtsuka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Higaita Ōtsuka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hokenoyama_kohun_aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hokenoyama Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]] [[File:Hashihaka kohun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|Hashihaka Kofun ({{国土航空写真}})]]
Six keyhole-shaped Kofuns are present within the group:
; {{ill|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|qid=Q11608505|lt=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun|1=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun}} : Located in Ōta, Sakurai City. Excavations revealed a surrounding moat approximately 20 meters wide. Artifacts include vermilion-painted wooden chicken figures, arc-patterned round boards, wooden tools, and intact Makimuku pottery. Carbon dating of cypress boards suggests a felling date around 200 CE.<ref>{{harvnb|Mitsutani|2000}}</ref>
; {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qid=Q11608503|lt=Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|1=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery, but the burial facility remains unexcavated. Exposed stone slabs suggest a pit-style stone chamber or box-shaped sarcophagus.
; {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qid=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|1=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} : Artifacts from the surrounding moat include wooden sword hilts, ritual fans, and Makimuku-period pottery. The layout forms an equilateral triangle with the Ishizuka and Yazuka mounds.
; {{ill|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun|qid=Q11528075|lt=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun|1=Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun}} : Located in Higashida, Sakurai City. Artifacts include pottery and wooden items. Excavations suggest the presence of jar coffins along the outer embankment.
; {{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|en|Hokenoyama Kofun|qid=Q11337664|lt=Hokenoyama Kofun|1=Hokenoyama Kofun}} : A wooden cist burial chamber was found containing a cypress boat-shaped coffin. Artifacts include bronze arrowheads, swords, and mirrors.<ref>{{harvnb|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|2008}}</ref>
; {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|qid=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} : Managed by the Imperial Household Agency. The moat contains artifacts from the 4th century, including ring stirrups.
